questions.optionB: Intestinal metaplasia of distal esophageal squamous epithelium (replaced by columnar epithelium with goblet cells) due to chronic GERD
questions.explanation: Barrett's esophagus: complication of chronic GERD → squamous epithelium of distal esophagus replaced by intestinal-type columnar epithelium WITH goblet cells (intestinal metaplasia). Pre-malignant: increased risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma (metaplasia → dysplasia → adenocarcinoma sequence). Requires endoscopic surveillance. Risk factors for GERD: obesity, hiatal hernia, alcohol, smoking, certain foods. Treatment: PPI + surveillance biopsies, ablation for dysplasia. Reference: Robbins — Barrett's Esophagus.

questions.questionText: Crohn's disease differs from ulcerative colitis in that Crohn's:
questions.optionB: Can affect any part of GI tract ('mouth to anus'), is transmural, has skip lesions, non-caseating granulomas, and fistulae
questions.explanation: Crohn's disease: any GI tract location (terminal ileum most common), transmural (all layers → fistulae, strictures), skip lesions (discontinuous), non-caseating granulomas (~50%), cobblestone mucosa, creeping fat, string sign on barium. UC: rectum always involved, extends proximally in continuous fashion, mucosa/submucosa only, crypt abscesses, pseudopolyps, bloody diarrhea, increased colorectal cancer risk (greater than Crohn's). Crohn's: association with perianal disease, calcium oxalate kidney stones. Reference: Robbins — IBD.

questions.questionText: Celiac disease is caused by immune-mediated damage to the small intestinal mucosa triggered by:
questions.explanation: Celiac disease (gluten-sensitive enteropathy): immune-mediated reaction to gliadin (gluten) in wheat, barley, rye → T cell-mediated damage to small intestinal mucosa (proximal > distal). Histology: villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, increased intraepithelial lymphocytes. Serology: anti-tTG IgA (most sensitive), anti-endomysial IgA (most specific), anti-deamidated gliadin. HLA-DQ2 (90%) or DQ8 association. Dermatitis herpetiformis: skin manifestation. Increased NHL and GI T-cell lymphoma risk. Reference: Robbins — Celiac Disease.

questions.questionText: The adenoma-carcinoma sequence in colorectal cancer involves:
questions.optionB: Normal epithelium → APC mutation → adenoma → KRAS → TP53/DCC loss → carcinoma
questions.explanation: Adenoma-carcinoma sequence (Vogelstein): stepwise accumulation of mutations. (1) APC loss (chromosome 5q) → adenomatous polyp initiation. (2) KRAS activation → adenoma growth. (3) SMAD4/DCC loss (18q) → progression. (4) TP53 loss (17p) → carcinoma. Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP): germline APC mutation → hundreds of polyps → 100% cancer risk. Lynch syndrome (HNPCC): mismatch repair gene defects (MSH2, MLH1) → microsatellite instability (MSI-H) pathway → right-sided colon cancer. Reference: Robbins — Colorectal Cancer.

questions.optionB: Absence of ganglion cells in the myenteric (Auerbach) and submucosal (Meissner) plexuses → functional obstruction
questions.explanation: Hirschsprung disease (congenital megacolon): failure of neural crest cell migration → aganglionosis of distal colon (rectosigmoid most common). Affected segment: ALWAYS includes rectum, extends proximally. Aganglionic segment: tonically contracted (narrow), proximal normal bowel dilates (megacolon). Presents in neonates: delayed passage of meconium, abdominal distension. Diagnosis: rectal suction biopsy (absent ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerve fibers). Associated with Down syndrome, RET gene mutations. Reference: Robbins — Hirschsprung.

questions.questionText: Peptic ulcer disease is most commonly caused by:
questions.optionB: H. pylori infection (most common cause) and NSAIDs
questions.explanation: Peptic ulcer: most commonly caused by H. pylori (~70% of duodenal, ~60% of gastric ulcers) and NSAIDs (~25%). H. pylori: gram-negative, urease-positive, lives in gastric mucus layer. Duodenal ulcer: most common, anterior wall (perforation risk), posterior wall (GDA bleeding). Gastric ulcer: lesser curvature (antrum). Complications: hemorrhage, perforation, obstruction (pyloric stenosis). H. pylori associations: MALToma, gastric adenocarcinoma. Triple therapy: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin. Reference: Robbins — PUD.

questions.questionText: Signet ring cells in the stomach are characteristic of:
questions.optionB: Diffuse-type gastric adenocarcinoma (linitis plastica, loss of E-cadherin/CDH1)
questions.explanation: Diffuse-type gastric adenocarcinoma: signet ring cells (intracellular mucin displaces nucleus to periphery). Infiltrates diffusely → linitis plastica (leather bottle stomach — thickened, rigid, non-distensible stomach wall). Loss of E-cadherin (CDH1 gene — cell adhesion molecule) → discohesive growth pattern. Worse prognosis than intestinal type. Krukenberg tumor: bilateral metastasis to ovaries (signet ring cells). Intestinal type: gland-forming, H. pylori associated, intestinal metaplasia, better prognosis. Reference: Robbins — Gastric Cancer.

questions.questionText: Appendicitis is most commonly caused by:
questions.optionB: Luminal obstruction (by fecalith in adults, lymphoid hyperplasia in children)
questions.explanation: Appendicitis: most common acute surgical abdomen. Pathogenesis: luminal obstruction → increased intraluminal pressure → ischemia → bacterial invasion → inflammation. Causes of obstruction: fecalith/appendicolith (most common in adults), lymphoid hyperplasia (most common in children — viral infections), foreign bodies, tumors (carcinoid — most common appendiceal tumor). Presents: periumbilical pain → migrates to RLF (McBurney's point), nausea, fever, leukocytosis. Complications: perforation → peritonitis, abscess. Reference: Robbins — Appendicitis.

questions.questionText: GIST (gastrointestinal stromal tumor) is characterized by mutation in:
questions.optionB: c-KIT (CD117) or PDGFRA — treated with imatinib
questions.explanation: GIST: most common mesenchymal tumor of the GI tract. Arises from interstitial cells of Cajal (pacemaker cells). Most common location: stomach (60%). c-KIT mutation (CD117, a tyrosine kinase): ~80% of GISTs → constitutively active KIT receptor. PDGFRA mutation: ~8%. Treatment: imatinib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting KIT/PDGFRA — paradigm of targeted therapy). Immunohistochemistry: CD117+, DOG1+. Can be discovered incidentally. Reference: Robbins — GIST.

questions.questionText: Carcinoid tumor of the appendix is the most common tumor of the appendix and arises from:
questions.explanation: Carcinoid tumors: well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors (NET). Appendix carcinoid: most common appendiceal tumor (usually <2 cm, benign behavior, found incidentally at appendectomy). Carcinoid syndrome: occurs when hepatic metastases release serotonin into systemic circulation → flushing, diarrhea, bronchoconstriction, right-sided heart disease (tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis). Diagnosis: elevated urinary 5-HIAA. Midgut carcinoids (ileum) most likely to metastasize. Reference: Robbins — Carcinoid Tumors.
